key_points: The atmosphere is divided into five layers starting from the earth's surface: Troposphere, Stratosphere, Mesosphere, Thermosphere, and Exosphere.
The Stratosphere contains a layer of ozone gas which protects us from the harmful effect of sun rays.
Air pressure is defined as the pressure exerted by the weight of air on the earth's surface.
Wind is the movement of air from high-pressure areas to low-pressure areas and is divided into Permanent, Seasonal, and Local winds.
Moisture in the air is called humidity; when water vapor rises, it cools and condenses to form clouds and eventually precipitation.
examples: Permanent Winds: The Trade winds, Westerlies, and Easterlies that blow constantly throughout the year.
Local Winds: 'Loo' (hot and dry wind of northern plains of India) or 'Land and Sea breeze'.
Rainfall Types: Convectional rainfall, Orographic (relief) rainfall, and Cyclonic rainfall.
Atmosphere Layers: The Thermosphere contains the Ionosphere which helps in radio transmission.
formulas: Temperature-Pressure Relation: High Temp $ightarrow$ Low Pressure (Air rises); Low Temp $ightarrow$ High Pressure (Air sinks)
Wind Direction = High Pressure Zone $ightarrow$ Low Pressure Zone
exam_tips: Remember that the Stratosphere is ideal for flying aeroplanes because it is free from clouds and weather phenomena.
Note that the Mesosphere is the layer where meteorites burn up on entering from space.
Understand that air always moves from high pressure to low pressure.
Identify that the Ionosphere reflects radio waves back to the earth.
